theme: Sculpture built from crossed plywood stacks
title: Crossed Stacks: Slicing a Plywood Sculpture at the Pin-Sheet Trade
strategies: trivial: keep the sculpture's own feature blocks as regions; for each one, use the first axis (X, then Y, then Z) whose cross-section fits a stock sheet, ignoring neighbours and the pin budget entirely (reproduces the checker's baseline, ~0.1)

greedy single-axis: try all three candidate slicing axes applied uniformly to the WHOLE sculpture, keep whichever is globally feasible and cheapest -- zero alignment pins, but a thin feature running the 'wrong' way relative to the chosen axis simply cannot be milled from any stock sheet, so a sculpture with arms in two different directions has no feasible global axis at all

strong isoperimetric allocation: let every block pick its own best-fitting axis independently, then spend the alignment-pin budget exactly where matching a neighbour's axis is worth it -- decoupling interior packing efficiency from boundary interface cost, which a single global axis (no freedom) or a per-block-only optimum (no budget awareness) can never reach
invalid: emit one region spanning the whole bounding grid, which necessarily swallows empty (non-solid) space and fails the reconstruction gate
